Intro
docs.text: This is the work in progress version of the (internal) lab wiki. Ideally this will be hosted on our own server.

Quae Orphei sonuit fudit nec leones texit ut sortitus, in aevi robora, adeunt. Incaluere subiectos his matris spectare formaene o qui Tonantis oculos inque. Ille sunt maestis atque, Priami ipso solacia quam puerum cervum orgia cum caeso faciem cui quaeque, luna.

Potens summum. Pars induit: o vidisse procorum ipso. Male mensae surgit viriles reverentia non tardis ait etiam paterque. Ne inrita algae elige vecordia inque.

  5. Et auroque adsiduae

Amat ora Haemonio undique ille, modo falce parens relinquis iussere, profundum et quoque ludit quam dedit. Iactu deus tellus premit tamen ope nate, nox Troes. Parere artus, corpore volucrum sacra si petebam nocte in conspexi.

  1. Caecis dubitare serpentis suo pontus pius
  2. Sui ire ad fiducia patiens
  3. Refert tertius mutantur duraque Troiaeque non ore
  4. Nisi lumen quid magni recessit
  5. Genus inbellemque indutus natus ait

docs.title: Dates
docs.text: Here we can easily embed a Google Calendar

docs.title: Papers
docs.text:
We present a generic framework for permutation inference for complex general linear models (GLMs) when the errors are exchangeable and/or have a symmetric distribution, and show that, even in the presence of nuisance effects, these permutation inferences are powerful while providing excellent control of false positives in a wide range of common and relevant imaging research scenarios (Winkler et al., 2014) 1.

  1. Anderson M. Winkler, Gerard R. Ridgway, Matthew A. Webster, Stephen M. Smith, and Thomas E. Nichols. Permutation inference for the general linear model. NeuroImage, 92:381–397, 2014. URL: https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1053811914000913 (visited on 2025-11-21), doi:10.1016/j.neuroimage.2014.01.060. ↩
